articles.title: EM@3AM: Topical Hemostatic Agents
articles.takeaway: When managing profuse bleeding from abrasions or superficial wounds where direct pressure fails, remember that different agents work via distinct mechanisms like factor delivery or mucoadhesion. While TXA shows promise for epistaxis, always consider the specific site and underlying pathophysiology when selecting a topical agent. This is a good quick reference to guide your initial choice before escalating care.
articles.shortSummary: This emDocs series provides a quick rundown of using topical hemostatic agents in the ED setting, which is super useful when direct pressure isn't enough to control bleeding from superficial wounds like abrasions.
articles.summary: This emDocs series provides a quick rundown of using topical hemostatic agents in the ED setting, which is super useful when direct pressure isn't enough to control bleeding from superficial wounds like abrasions. The review smartly categorizes these agents based on how they work—whether it’s by delivering concentrated clotting factors, sticking to the tissue (mucoadhesion), or actively promoting coagulation. For example, the material highlights that topical TXA seems particularly effective for stopping acute epistaxis compared to other available options. Overall, it gives a practical framework for selecting an agent based on the bleeding source and mechanism.

articles.title: Reducing Hyperoxemia in Mechanically Ventilated Emergency Department Patients: A Before-and-After Study
articles.takeaway: When titrating oxygen in ventilated ED patients, be aware that implementing standard hyperoxemia reduction protocols may increase the overall risk of hypoxemia. While guidelines are useful tools, they might push you toward an unsafe lower end of the spectrum; therefore, maintain a high index of suspicion for underlying respiratory compromise even when aiming to reduce FiO2.
articles.shortSummary: This before-and-after study assessed the impact of implementing a specific oxygen titration guideline in mechanically ventilated emergency department patients regarding their oxygenation status.
articles.summary: This before-and-after study assessed the impact of implementing a specific oxygen titration guideline in mechanically ventilated emergency department patients regarding their oxygenation status. The authors found that adopting this guideline was effective at reducing the incidence of severe hyperoxemia among the cohort. However, this benefit came at a cost, as the implementation was associated with an increase in overall hypoxemia. Notably, the rate of severe hypoxemia did not appear to change following the guideline's adoption. The conclusion emphasizes that while guidelines can successfully curb excessive oxygen delivery, they might inadvertently elevate the risk for lower oxygen levels, suggesting a need for more definitive research on optimal targets.

articles.title: A multi-center retrospective cohort study of SUGAmmadex for neuromuscular blockade reversal in the emergency department: SUGARED study - on behalf of EMPHARM-NET Investigators
articles.takeaway: When using sugammadex to reverse rocuronium in TBI or sICH patients, be prepared to reassess both the GCS and the patient's analgesic requirements post-reversal. The observed association suggests monitoring for any unexpected neurological dips or changes in sedation needs is prudent, though this study was retrospective and observational.
articles.shortSummary: This multi-center retrospective cohort study, the SUGARED study, looked at using sugammadex to reverse rocuronium blockade specifically within the emergency department setting for patients who had experienced traumatic…
articles.summary: This multi-center retrospective cohort study, the SUGARED study, looked at using sugammadex to reverse rocuronium blockade specifically within the emergency department setting for patients who had experienced traumatic brain injury or symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage. The authors found that administering sugammadex was associated with significant changes in both the patient's Glasgow Coma Scale and their need for analgesia/sedation management. This suggests that simply reversing the neuromuscular block might have downstream effects on neurological status or pain control that warrant closer attention when managing these complex trauma patients.

articles.title: Paramedic-assessed quality of bystander CPR is associated with survival in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest
articles.takeaway: When considering resuscitation efforts in the field, remember that optimizing compression quality is a higher yield target than just documenting bystander involvement. Focus educational and procedural efforts on ensuring providers can maintain adequate depth and rate during compressions. Be mindful that this finding suggests systemic process improvement is needed rather than just individual provider education.
articles.shortSummary: This piece emphasizes a critical shift in understanding bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation (bCPR) following out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
articles.summary: This piece emphasizes a critical shift in understanding bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation (bCPR) following out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. The core message is that the *quality* of compressions delivered by bystanders matters significantly more for survival outcomes than merely confirming that CPR was performed at all. This suggests that simply having someone start chest compressions isn't enough; the technical proficiency and adherence to high-quality guidelines are paramount. Therefore, the authors strongly direct attention toward systemic improvements within EMS protocols and policy development aimed specifically at enhancing the mechanics of bCPR delivery.

articles.title: Sex Differences in OHCA Outcomes Are Driven by Arrest Circumstances Rather Than Different EMS Treatment: - a nationwide registry study from Norway 2015-2024
articles.takeaway: When reviewing sex-based differences in OHCA survival data, remember that observed disparities are unlikely to be explained by differential EMS management. Focus your clinical attention on identifying non-treatment related confounders—such as baseline comorbidities or arrest setting—as the primary drivers of outcome variance between sexes. However, proceed with caution, as this study only establishes an association and does not prove causation for any specific factor.
articles.shortSummary: This large Norwegian registry study analyzing Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA) outcomes from 2015 to 2024 sought to understand the sex differences observed in survival rates.
articles.summary: This large Norwegian registry study analyzing Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA) outcomes from 2015 to 2024 sought to understand the sex differences observed in survival rates. While the authors noted significant disparities between men and women regarding baseline characteristics, the number of events survived, and even 30-day survival, they specifically tested whether these variations were attributable to differences in prehospital Emergency Medical Services (EMS) care provided. The key finding was that despite observing sex-based differences across multiple outcome measures, the type or extent of EMS treatment administered did not appear to be related to the patient's biological sex. This suggests that if sex is a factor influencing OHCA outcomes, it likely stems from underlying physiological or environmental factors rather than variations in standard prehospital protocols.

articles.title: Association of Rescue Breathing With Outcomes in Adult Suffocation-Related Cardiac Arrest
articles.takeaway: Don't overemphasize the difference between compression-only versus ventilated bystander CPR when managing suffocation arrests, as this data suggests it may not be the primary driver of neurological recovery. Focus instead on ensuring high-quality initial care and recognizing that multiple prehospital factors likely contribute more significantly to a good outcome than just the mode of resuscitation provided by bystanders. Keep optimizing overall scene management.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study examined whether using compression-only versus ventilatory bystander CPR makes a difference in neurological outcomes following cardiac arrest due to suffocation in adults.
articles.summary: This retrospective cohort study examined whether using compression-only versus ventilatory bystander CPR makes a difference in neurological outcomes following cardiac arrest due to suffocation in adults. The main takeaway from the analysis is that the specific type of bystander resuscitation provided did not correlate with better neurological recovery rates. This suggests that while we are often taught about optimizing CPR components, simply knowing whether ventilation was added by a lay rescuer might not be the critical factor driving good outcomes in this specific population. The authors caution that other prehospital variables likely play a more significant role in determining how well these patients recover.

articles.title: PulmCrit: Is oseltamivir assassinating ICU patients?
articles.takeaway: Given the preliminary signal suggesting a near 98% likelihood of increased mortality, exercise extreme caution when using oseltamivir in critically ill patients. While this is based on initial analysis from REMAP-CAP, it mandates pausing routine use until more comprehensive data are available for review. Do not change established care pathways based solely on these preliminary figures.
articles.shortSummary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data regarding the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming.
articles.summary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data regarding the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming. The primary analysis suggests a substantial association between oseltamivir administration and increased mortality risk within this vulnerable population. Specifically, the reported likelihood of causing increased death is stated to be 98.3%. Given these preliminary results, it warrants immediate attention from any clinician considering antiviral prophylaxis or treatment with oseltamivir in an ICU setting. A thorough review of the underlying data is strongly recommended before making any changes to current institutional protocols.

articles.title: Infant with seizure
articles.takeaway: When evaluating neonatal/infant head trauma with concerning signs, remember that ultrasound is excellent for characterizing intracranial collections; look specifically for the displacement of subarachnoid vessels away from the collection to favor a subdural etiology. While this case was straightforward, always maintain a high index of suspicion for underlying pathology even if initial findings seem benign, and consider the mechanism of injury thoroughly.
articles.shortSummary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting with concerning neurological signs following minor trauma, specifically differentiating between subdural hema…
articles.summary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting with concerning neurological signs following minor trauma, specifically differentiating between subdural hematomas (SDH), subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH), and hygromas. The 5-month-old patient presented after a fall with irritability and tonic posturing, prompting imaging that revealed bilateral, heterogeneous, multilayered collections over the fontanelle. Crucially, the ultrasound findings, including color Doppler evidence of displaced subarachnoid vessels away from the collection, strongly supported an SDH diagnosis rather than SAH or hygroma. This underscores how focused bedside assessment can guide initial management in pediatric trauma.

articles.title: ECG Cases 62 – ACLS Arrhythmia Pitfalls, Part 5: Stable Narrow Complex Tachycardias
articles.takeaway: When faced with a stable narrow complex tachycardia, don't assume the rhythm based solely on rate or regularity; systematically differentiate between sinus, A-fib, flutter, and SVT using all available leads. Always keep secondary causes—like electrolyte abnormalities or drugs—in your differential before committing to an ablation or cardioversion strategy. Remember that careful interpretation trumps immediate intervention.
articles.shortSummary: This installment of the ECG Cases series tackles the common but tricky pitfalls encountered when interpreting stable narrow complex tachycardias in the emergency setting.
articles.summary: This installment of the ECG Cases series tackles the common but tricky pitfalls encountered when interpreting stable narrow complex tachycardias in the emergency setting. The resource uses eight real-world case examples to guide readers through differentiating between sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). It's a valuable refresher because these rhythms can often mimic each other on ECG, leading to diagnostic uncertainty and potentially inappropriate management decisions. Beyond just pattern recognition, the discussion stresses the importance of considering secondary causes for the tachyarrhythmia, which is crucial for comprehensive patient care in the ED.

articles.title: Predicting Echocardiography Findings in Adults Presenting to the Emergency Department With Syncope: An External Validation of the ROMEO Score
articles.takeaway: If you calculate a zero ROMEO score in an ED syncope patient, you can feel quite confident that their TTE is likely to be negative. Use this score as a risk stratification tool to safely de-escalate the need for echo testing in those who are low probability for structural heart disease. Remember that while NPV is high, clinical context always dictates management.
articles.shortSummary: This paper focuses on externally validating the utility of the ROMEO score in predicting echocardiography findings among adult patients presenting to the emergency department with syncope.
articles.summary: This paper focuses on externally validating the utility of the ROMEO score in predicting echocardiography findings among adult patients presenting to the emergency department with syncope. The core finding is that a zero ROMEO score exhibits very strong predictive performance, boasting a reported Negative Predictive Value of 98.6%. Essentially, this tool aims to help triage which syncope patients are unlikely to have significant structural cardiac abnormalities revealed by a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE). This suggests the score could be valuable for guiding diagnostic workup and potentially reducing unnecessary imaging in low-risk cohorts.

articles.title: Acute urinary retention presenting as inferior pseudo-STEMI: A case report
articles.takeaway: When you see isolated inferior ST-segment elevation without clear cardiac triggers or hemodynamic compromise, always consider massive urinary retention. Simple decompression via catheterization is often diagnostic and therapeutic, resolving the changes completely even if the patient appears stable otherwise. Don't let a 'normal' vital sign profile rule out this straightforward cause.
articles.shortSummary: This case report highlights a classic but sometimes overlooked scenario where massive bladder distension can generate ECG changes mimicking an inferior STEMI, presenting as inferior ST-segment elevation.
articles.summary: This case report highlights a classic but sometimes overlooked scenario where massive bladder distension can generate ECG changes mimicking an inferior STEMI, presenting as inferior ST-segment elevation. The authors detail a patient whose initial workup suggested an acute coronary syndrome until simple Foley catheterization resolved the ST-elevation completely. What's most striking is that this resolution occurred without any associated signs of hemodynamic instability, such as tachycardia or hypertension, which might otherwise prompt further cardiac workup. This underscores that while inferior ST changes are highly suggestive of NSTEMI/STEMI, a thorough history and physical exam must include assessing for urinary retention in the differential diagnosis. It serves as a good reminder to keep bladder pathology high on the list when faced with reversible ECG abnormalities.

editions.label: 27 June 2026
editions.headline: Fluid Wars, Oxygen Titration, and PE Triage: Key Shifts in Critical Care Practice
editions.summary:
The resuscitation debate continues to yield caution; the ARISE-FLUIDS data suggest that whether you favor early vasopressors with restricted fluids or liberal initial fluid boluses, neither strategy demonstrated a clear advantage for 90-day survival in septic shock. This lack of definitive superiority should temper assumptions about aggressive fluid management at the bedside.

Meanwhile, optimizing oxygenation requires vigilance: while implementing formal guidelines to curb severe hyperoxemia is beneficial, one must be acutely aware that this can inadvertently shift the balance toward inadequate oxygen delivery. Furthermore, when managing low-risk acute PE in the DOAC era, evidence suggests hospitalization may not confer a survival benefit over outpatient management for sPESI 0 or 1 patients.

Beyond shock and oxygenation, consider the procedural details: When performing RSI, remember that regardless of whether you use rocuronium or succinylcholine, proactively planning for adequate post-intubation analgesia is paramount. Finally, in pediatric OHCA, elevated lactate measured within six hours of ROSC warrants consideration as a potential biomarker for poorer long-term outcomes.
editions.notes: Today’s reading set emphasizes that guideline implementation requires constant vigilance regarding unintended physiological consequences.

articles.title: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial
articles.takeaway: The ARISE-FLUIDS data suggest caution when titrating between early vasopressor initiation with restricted fluids versus liberal initial fluid resuscitation. Since the trial did not show improved 90-day survival with either strategy, clinicians should approach these protocols without assuming a definitive benefit to one over the other at the bedside.
articles.shortSummary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two distinct resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongsid…
articles.summary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two distinct resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongside restricted fluid administration, while the other received higher fluid volumes with later initiation of vasopressors. The primary finding reported is that neither of these approaches demonstrated superiority over the other regarding the rate of survival at day 90. This lack of difference suggests that current resuscitation protocols involving aggressive fluid management versus early pressor use may not yield a clear advantage in terms of long-term outcomes for this critically ill population.

articles.title: Rocuronium vs Succinylcholine for RSI: Awareness, Paralysis, and Post-Intubation Sedation
articles.takeaway: When choosing a paralytic for RSI, remember that while rocuronium might delay starting post-intubation sedation compared to succinylcholine, the absolute difference is small. Regardless of the agent used, always plan ahead for adequate analgesia and sedation *before* intubating to mitigate the risk of intraoperative awareness.
articles.shortSummary: This review discusses a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and ana…
articles.summary: This review discusses a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and analgesia needs. The core finding suggests that while rocuronium was linked to delayed initiation of post-intubation sedation compared to succinylcholine, the magnitude of this difference appeared small, leading to uncertainty regarding its true clinical significance. A key concern raised is the potential for awareness during paralysis when utilizing longer-acting agents like rocuronium. Consequently, the authors recommend proactively planning for adequate post-intubation sedation and analgesia before proceeding with RSI, particularly when using drugs with prolonged effects.

articles.title: Reducing Hyperoxemia in Mechanically Ventilated Emergency Department Patients: A Before-and-After Study
articles.takeaway: When implementing oxygen titration protocols, be mindful that reducing hyperoxia might increase the risk of developing hypoxemia. Don't assume a guideline will optimize oxygenation without careful monitoring; maintaining appropriate FiO2 and PEEP remains critical. Further research is needed to establish safe, optimal targets for all ventilation parameters.
articles.shortSummary: This before-and-after study assessed the impact of implementing a formal oxygen titration guideline in the emergency department setting for mechanically ventilated patients.
articles.summary: This before-and-after study assessed the impact of implementing a formal oxygen titration guideline in the emergency department setting for mechanically ventilated patients. The authors found that adopting this structured approach successfully reduced the incidence of severe hyperoxemia among the cohort. However, this benefit came at a cost, as the implementation was associated with an increase in overall hypoxemia. Notably, the rate of severe hypoxemia did not change following guideline adoption. Overall, the findings suggest that while guidelines can effectively curb excessive oxygen delivery, they may inadvertently shift the balance toward inadequate oxygenation.

articles.title: Maintenance of prehospital anaesthesia using an intermittent bolus regime in blunt trauma patients with a high GCS and hemodynamic reserve: a retrospective cohort study
articles.takeaway: When maintaining anesthesia prehospital in stable blunt trauma patients (GCS ≥ 9), be mindful that intermittent boluses alone can lead to highly variable cumulative drug exposure. Consider supplementing or adjusting your technique if you suspect dosing variability might compromise anesthetic depth, as relying solely on this method risks sub-therapeutic levels. This suggests a need for more standardized infusion protocols over purely bolus-based maintenance.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study examined the practice of maintaining anesthesia in prehospital blunt trauma patients who were hemodynamically stable and had a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of 9 or greater, specifically focus…
articles.summary: This retrospective cohort study examined the practice of maintaining anesthesia in prehospital blunt trauma patients who were hemodynamically stable and had a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of 9 or greater, specifically focusing on those managed with an intermittent bolus-only regimen. The authors concluded that relying solely on intermittent boluses for anesthetic maintenance introduces significant variability into the total cumulative doses administered to these patients. This inherent dosing unpredictability raises a genuine concern regarding the potential for plasma levels to fall below therapeutic thresholds in the field setting. While the cohort was relatively homogeneous, the core finding points toward an issue with dose consistency when using this specific maintenance strategy.

articles.title: A multi-center retrospective cohort study of SUGAmmadex for neuromuscular blockade reversal in the emergency department: SUGARED study - on behalf of EMPHARM-NET Investigators
articles.takeaway: Be mindful that rocuronium reversal with sugammadex in TBI/sICH patients isn't just about muscle tone; monitor closely for changes in GCS and analgesic requirements post-reversal. Given the association found, consider reassessing sedation needs rather than assuming a stable baseline state immediately after block reversal.

articles.summary: This multi-center retrospective cohort study, the SUGARED study, examined the use of sugammadex to reverse rocuronium blockade specifically within the emergency department setting for patients who had sustained traumatic brain injury or intracranial hemorrhage. The authors found a notable association between administering sugammadex and subsequent changes in both Glasgow Coma Scale scores and the need for analgesia/sedation. This suggests that simply reversing the neuromuscular block might have downstream effects on neurological status or pain management requirements beyond just muscle function. While the finding is interesting, it is important to remember this was a retrospective analysis.

articles.title: Critical Care Evidence Updates – May 2026
articles.takeaway: For CVAD management, keep the evidence regarding 4% Tetrasodium EDTA for infection prophylaxis in mind, but remember this is part of a broader discussion. When managing male ICU patients with urinary catheters, consider implementing securement bands proactively to mitigate meatal pressure injury risk. These updates suggest incorporating these specific, low-cost interventions into routine rounds.
articles.shortSummary: This month's critical care update synthesizes several recent findings, touching on device-related complications, delirium management, and basic patient care measures.
articles.summary: This month's critical care update synthesizes several recent findings, touching on device-related complications, delirium management, and basic patient care measures. Notably, there is discussion around the use of 4% Tetrasodium EDTA for preventing central venous access device (CVAD) site infections, alongside reviewing nurse-led protocols for delirium prevention which suggests a role for non-physician staff in these areas. For routine bedside practice, the literature specifically draws attention to using urinary catheter securement bands as a measure to prevent meatal pressure injuries in male intensive care unit patients. Overall, it provides a broad overview of evolving best practices across several core critical care domains.

articles.title: Association of Serum Lactate with Outcome After Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Secondary Analysis of the Therapeutic Hypothermia After Pediatric Cardiac Arrest (THAPCA) Trial
articles.takeaway: Elevated lactate measured within 6 hours of ROSC should prompt consideration of poorer long-term prognosis following pediatric OHCA. This suggests that serial lactate monitoring could be useful for risk stratification, but clinicians should interpret this alongside established neurological and physiological scores, as the utility is currently suggested rather than definitively proven.
articles.shortSummary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically look at serum lactate levels as a predictor of long-term outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
articles.summary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically look at serum lactate levels as a predictor of long-term outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. The key finding reported is that elevated lactate concentrations measured within the first six hours after return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) correlated with poorer one-year survival and unfavorable neurological outcomes. This suggests that lactate may serve as an adjunct biomarker for early risk stratification in this critically ill population. While promising, it's important to remember this is a secondary analysis drawing conclusions about prognostic utility from existing trial data.

articles.title: Critical care delivery models in emergency departments: a systematic review of the literature and meta-analysis of related outcome effects
articles.takeaway: If your facility is considering optimizing its approach to managing critically ill patients in the ED, these findings suggest that establishing a dedicated critical care zone, like an ED-ICU, warrants consideration. This model appears linked to better outcomes, specifically lower rates of both ICU transfer and overall hospital stay duration compared to less structured models. Remember this is based on meta-analysis, so local resource availability must guide implementation.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized the literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesized the literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes. The authors categorized several approaches, with dedicated critical care areas, such as an ED-ICU setup, being the most frequently documented model. The quantitative analysis performed suggests a tangible benefit to implementing these specialized zones. Specifically, compared to other established CC-ED delivery models, dedicating space and resources within the ED for critical care appears associated with reductions in both subsequent ICU admission rates and overall hospital length of stay for critically ill patients presenting through the department.

While further review is necessary, these findings strongly suggest that current guidelines may need re-evaluation for this drug class in the ICU setting. Do not change established protocols without reviewing the full dataset and institutional consensus.

articles.title: Infant with seizure
articles.takeaway: When evaluating an infant with concerning signs after trauma, remember that bedside cranial ultrasound can be instrumental in localizing intracranial collections. Finding multilayered fluid collections with associated vascular changes strongly favors a subdural collection over SAH or hygromas. However, always correlate these imaging findings with the clinical picture and consider the limitations of ultrasound in this age group.
articles.shortSummary: This case report details the management of a 5-month-old infant presenting with concerning neurological signs, including irritability and tonic posturing following a minor fall.
articles.summary: This case report details the management of a 5-month-old infant presenting with concerning neurological signs, including irritability and tonic posturing following a minor fall. Key physical exam findings included ecchymoses and a tense anterior fontanelle. The crucial diagnostic step involved bedside cranial ultrasonography, which visualized bilateral heterogeneous, multilayered collections. These ultrasound findings were interpreted by the authors as highly suggestive of subdural hematomas (SDH), effectively differentiating them from other potential diagnoses like subarachnoid hemorrhage or hygromas. The use of color Doppler to show vessel displacement away from the cortical surface further supported the diagnosis of SDH.

articles.title: ACMT Toxicology Visual Pearl – Black Vomit, Big Problem
articles.takeaway: When a patient presents with black emesis and a garlicky or fishy odor, immediately consider ingestion of zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides. Be hypervigilant for signs of impending cardiovascular collapse, as profound shock can develop rapidly despite an initially stable appearance. Supportive care focusing on airway management and circulatory support is paramount until definitive toxicology workup can be completed.
articles.shortSummary: This visual pearl highlights the critical toxidrome associated with ingesting zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides, which are often encountered as black pellets.
articles.summary: This visual pearl highlights the critical toxidrome associated with ingesting zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides, which are often encountered as black pellets. Ingestion leads to a rapid onset of symptoms characterized by nausea and vomiting dark emesis that has a distinct garlicky or fishy odor. The underlying mechanism involves the release of phosphine gas, which is highly toxic because it inhibits mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, leading to significant cardiovascular compromise. Clinically, this presentation can be deceptive; patients may appear stable initially but are at high risk for rapid decompensation into profound shock. Recognizing the source and the associated pathophysiology is key to early management.

articles.title: ECG Cases 62 – ACLS Arrhythmia Pitfalls, Part 5: Stable Narrow Complex Tachycardias
articles.takeaway: When faced with a stable narrow complex tachycardia, don't rely solely on rate or regular rhythm; systematically evaluate for atrial flutter versus true SVT, paying close attention to any subtle waveform changes. Always maintain a high index of suspicion for secondary causes like electrolyte abnormalities or drug effects before committing to cardioversion or rate control agents.

articles.title: Predicting Echocardiography Findings in Adults Presenting to the Emergency Department With Syncope: An External Validation of the ROMEO Score
articles.takeaway: For stable patients presenting with syncope, consider using the ROMEO score to stratify TTE necessity; a zero score suggests a very low likelihood of significant cardiac findings. This could help streamline resource use by safely withholding an echo in appropriately low-risk individuals. Remember that this is a predictive tool, and clinical judgment must always guide the decision, especially if the patient presentation remains atypical.

articles.summary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting with syncope will have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE). The core finding is that a zero ROMEO score exhibits remarkably strong predictive performance, boasting a 98.6% Negative Predictive Value. Therefore, the authors argue this score can serve as a useful triage mechanism in the busy emergency department setting. It allows clinicians to potentially de-escalate care by avoiding unnecessary echo testing for low-risk syncope presentations.

articles.title: Acute urinary retention presenting as inferior pseudo-STEMI: A case report
articles.takeaway: When you see isolated, reversible inferior ST-segment elevation without clear cardiac etiology or hemodynamic instability, strongly consider massive urinary retention. Decompression is often curative and simple; however, remember that even if the ECG normalizes quickly, a thorough workup for true ischemia should still be completed.
articles.shortSummary: This case report details a scenario where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior STEMI, which is a classic but potentially missed diagnosis in the ED.
articles.summary: This case report details a scenario where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior STEMI, which is a classic but potentially missed diagnosis in the ED. The authors highlight that simple decompression with a Foley catheter was sufficient to resolve the ST-segment elevation completely, even in the absence of typical signs of hemodynamic compromise such as tachycardia or hypertension. This underscores that profound urinary retention can cause significant, reversible electrical changes mimicking acute coronary syndrome findings. It serves as an excellent reminder for all emergency physicians to keep this diagnosis high on the differential list when encountering unexplained inferior ST-elevation.

articles.title: Outcomes of Acute PE Treated With DOACs in the Veterans Affairs Health System: A Retrospective Cohort Study
articles.takeaway: For your low-risk sPESI patients (0 or 1), you can feel more confident discharging them rather than admitting them solely for acute PE management, as hospitalization didn't improve 30-day mortality. Remember that this finding is specific to the DOAC era and does not negate the need for appropriate anticoagulation initiation. Always ensure there isn't another compelling reason requiring admission.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S.
articles.summary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S. population presenting with acute pulmonary embolism (PE) who were managed with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs). The authors stratified their analysis based on the Wells' criteria equivalent, specifically using the sPESI score. The key finding was that for patients classified as low risk, meaning those with an sPESI of 0 or 1, receiving inpatient hospitalization did not appear to confer a survival benefit compared to being managed in the outpatient setting. This suggests that current practice may be over-hospitalizing a significant portion of low-risk PE patients.

articles.title: No Pause, No Problem? Using Doppler Ultrasound to Detect ROSC Without Pausing Compressions for Pulse Check
articles.takeaway: If you are concerned about pulse checks compromising CPR quality, remember that Doppler assessment of femoral artery waveforms during compressions may offer a way to screen for ROSC without pausing chest compressions. While this concept is promising for workflow improvement, its utility should be viewed as an adjunct tool rather than a replacement for established protocols; always maintain high-quality compressions until definitive rhythm confirmation.
articles.shortSummary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during CPR, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality chest compressions.
articles.summary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during CPR, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality chest compressions. The core concept explored is leveraging continuous femoral arterial Doppler ultrasound waveforms obtained *during* active compressions to detect signs of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). Instead of pausing compressions for a traditional palpation or line check, the authors evaluate if detecting pulsatility and anterograde flow signals provides an accurate surrogate marker for true cardiac activity. This approach suggests a potential paradigm shift in resuscitation protocols by allowing continuous monitoring without sacrificing compression quality.

articles.title: Status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy presenting to the emergency department: Predictors and mortality risk
articles.takeaway: Given the high incidence and associated mortality risk, consider proactively stratifying epilepsy patients presenting with altered mental status. Pay extra attention to those who are elderly, have known metabolic issues, or whose presentation suggests an underlying CNS source for potential SE. This approach can guide timely escalation of care.
articles.shortSummary: This study provides a local perspective on the incidence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) in epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department, noting that SE was quite common, affecting nearly one-quart…
articles.summary: This study provides a local perspective on the incidence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) in epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department, noting that SE was quite common, affecting nearly one-quarter of this cohort. Crucially, the authors highlight a strong association between developing SE and increased in-hospital mortality. The findings suggest that risk stratification is warranted for these patients upon presentation. Specifically, they point toward older age, metabolic derangements, or suspicion of central nervous system infection as key areas to focus on during initial assessment.

articles.title: Risk of obstructive acute kidney injury: derivation and internal validation of a risk stratification tree
articles.takeaway: For a patient presenting with AKI where urological etiology is suspected, consider using this risk stratification tool if available; acute flank or hypogastric pain strongly elevates suspicion for obstruction. Remember that the model's utility is currently limited to internal validation, so do not rely on it as definitive proof at the bedside. Regardless of the calculated risk score, a thorough physical exam and appropriate imaging remain paramount.
articles.shortSummary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting.
articles.summary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting. The study utilized a retrospective derivation and internal validation cohort approach on adult patients presenting with AKI of any KDIGO stage. The model stratifies risk based on clinical presentation; specifically, patients reporting lumbar, flank, or hypogastric pain are categorized as 'high risk,' showing an associated obstructive AKI risk around 55%. A 'moderate risk' group is defined for those lacking acute pain but possessing specific histories such as prior urinary tract surgery or abdominal cancer. The authors note that the model achieved high sensitivity in identifying low-risk patients, though they caution that external and prospective validation is necessary before clinical implementation.

articles.title: Airway Foreign Bodies
articles.takeaway: When considering an airway foreign body, remember to maintain a high level of suspicion across age groups, especially in the very young and elderly. For initial management in adults, flexible bronchoscopy is the preferred first step unless you suspect a large or deeply impacted object requiring rigid instrumentation. Always consider the potential for subtle presentations that might otherwise be missed.
articles.shortSummary: This overview provides a comprehensive look at airway foreign bodies (AFBs), emphasizing that these can be potentially life-threatening emergencies.
articles.summary: This overview provides a comprehensive look at airway foreign bodies (AFBs), emphasizing that these can be potentially life-threatening emergencies. A key point highlighted is the demographic predilection, noting that while they affect various groups, suspicion should remain high in both children under five and adults over sixty-five. The article details the pathophysiology, potential complications, and diagnostic workup for these objects. Management strategies are differentiated based on object size and location; flexible bronchoscopy is generally recommended as the initial approach for most adult cases, whereas rigid bronchoscopy retains its critical role for larger or more proximal impactions. Given that presentations can be subtle, maintaining a high index of suspicion remains paramount.

articles.title: Real-time identification of aetiology in patients able to undergo transoesophageal echocardiography with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in China: a prospective, single-centre exploratory study
articles.takeaway: TEE appears to be a rapid and feasible tool for identifying potential causes during CPR in NT-OHCA patients, with acute aortic dissection being a notable finding here. However, remember this is an exploratory series lacking a control arm; therefore, do not change your standard resuscitation algorithm based on this data alone. Use it as supportive information while awaiting definitive diagnostic confirmation.
articles.shortSummary: This single-center study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients presenting with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA) in China.
articles.summary: This single-center study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients presenting with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA) in China. The authors found that TEE successfully provided diagnostic images in all 43 eligible patients who underwent the procedure, suggesting its feasibility during active resuscitation efforts. Reversible etiologies were identified in 39% of cases analyzed, with acute aortic dissection being the most frequently diagnosed condition at 50%. While TEE guided interventions occurred in over half the identified cases, the study noted that there was no significant difference in return of spontaneous circulation or 28-day survival rates between those whose cause was identified versus those whose cause remained unknown. Crucially, the authors emphasized that due to the lack of a comparator group, these findings warrant further controlled investigation.

editions.label: 26 June 2026
editions.headline: Fluid Wars, Late Strokes, and Streamlining ED Care
editions.summary:
The data on septic shock management continues to temper enthusiasm for maximal intervention. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial results are a stark reminder that aggressive initial fluid loading combined with early vasopressors did not confer a survival benefit over more conservative strategies, urging us to approach resuscitation titration with genuine caution.

Shifting focus across the spectrum of acute care, we see utility in risk stratification tools. For syncope workups, the ROMEO score offers a practical way to safely de-escalate unnecessary echocardiograms when the predictive value is high for low-risk patients. Similarly, for PE management, low-risk patients (sPESI 0 or 1) may not require hospitalization for 30-day survival benefit, suggesting pathways ripe for outpatient optimization.

Beyond individual algorithms, systemic improvements are emerging: dedicated ED-ICU zones show promise in reducing overall hospital length of stay. Furthermore, while late-window stroke remains challenging, meta-analyses suggest that when EVT is applied judiciously to highly selected patients, salvageable benefit might still exist. Today’s reading set demands we balance the allure of aggressive intervention with emerging evidence favoring targeted, resource-conscious care.
editions.notes: Keep your index of suspicion high for underlying mechanical issues like urinary obstruction in any patient presenting with shock.

articles.title: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial
articles.takeaway: The ARISE-FLUIDS data suggest caution regarding the routine implementation of both early vasopressors and high-volume fluids simultaneously in septic shock. When managing a patient in septic shock, remember that aggressive initial fluid resuscitation combined with immediate vasopressor support did not confer a survival benefit over more restricted fluid strategies. Always consider the underlying pathophysiology when titrating these interventions.
articles.shortSummary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongside restric…
articles.summary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongside restricted fluid administration, while the other received higher fluid volumes with later initiation of vasopressors. The primary finding reported is that neither management strategy resulted in a statistically greater number of days alive at day 90 when compared head-to-head. This suggests that current resuscitation guidelines advocating for aggressive initial fluid loading combined with early vasopressor use may lack definitive superiority over more conservative approaches.

articles.title: Endovascular intervention for acute stroke in the very late window: A meta-analysis of 90-day mRS and procedural outcomes
articles.takeaway: For patients presenting significantly outside the standard time window, consider EVT only after rigorous selection criteria are met, as the benefit appears restricted to specific subgroups. Remember that while improved functional outcomes and reduced mortality were suggested, the overall evidence base remains somewhat heterogeneous across outcome measures. Proceed with caution and ensure comprehensive imaging supports intervention in these challenging late-window cases.
articles.shortSummary: This meta-analysis synthesized data regarding the use of endovascular therapy (EVT) for acute ischemic stroke occurring in very late windows, specifically beyond the initial 24 hours.
articles.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized data regarding the use of endovascular therapy (EVT) for acute ischemic stroke occurring in very late windows, specifically beyond the initial 24 hours. The authors concluded that when applied judiciously to selected patients, EVT can indeed offer benefits by improving functional outcomes and reducing all-cause mortality. However, the analysis also stressed that patient selection is paramount, suggesting that the benefit is not universal across all late-presenting stroke cohorts. Overall, while the potential for salvage remains, clinicians must interpret these findings with caution due to the mixed nature of the underlying data.

articles.title: Rocuronium vs Succinylcholine for RSI: Awareness, Paralysis, and Post-Intubation Sedation
articles.takeaway: When choosing a paralytic for RSI, remember that while rocuronium might theoretically delay post-intubation sedation compared to succinylcholine, the magnitude of this difference is clinically debatable. Regardless of agent choice, always plan ahead for adequate analgesia and sedation *before* intubating, as maintaining optimal patient comfort immediately post-RSI remains paramount.
articles.shortSummary: This review summarizes a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and an…
articles.summary: This review summarizes a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and analgesia needs. The authors noted that while rocuronium appeared associated with delayed initiation of post-intubation sedation and analgesia compared to succinylcholine, they cautioned that the absolute median differences observed were quite small, casting doubt on definitive clinical significance. A key concern raised is the potential for awareness during paralysis when utilizing longer-acting agents like rocuronium. Given these nuances, the authors recommend proactively planning for adequate post-intubation sedation and analgesia before proceeding with RSI, particularly when using agents with prolonged effects.

articles.title: Association of Serum Lactate with Outcome After Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Secondary Analysis of the Therapeutic Hypothermia After Pediatric Cardiac Arrest (THAPCA) Trial
articles.takeaway: If you encounter a child who has experienced OHCA and achieves ROSC, consider obtaining serial lactate measurements within the first six hours as part of your risk stratification. Higher levels are associated with worse one-year outcomes, suggesting it could guide more aggressive post-resuscitation care or further workup. However, this finding is based on an observational secondary analysis, so interpret these results cautiously.
articles.shortSummary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically assess the utility of serum lactate levels in predicting outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
articles.summary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically assess the utility of serum lactate levels in predicting outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The key finding reported is a correlation between elevated lactate concentrations measured within the first six hours after return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) and poorer one-year survival. This suggests that lactate may serve as an adjunct biomarker for early neuroprognostication and overall mortality risk in this critically ill pediatric population. While the association was noted, it's important to remember this is a secondary analysis drawing conclusions from existing trial data.

articles.title: Critical care delivery models in emergency departments: a systematic review of the literature and meta-analysis of related outcome effects
articles.takeaway: When managing critically ill patients in the ED, structuring care within a dedicated critical care area like an ED-ICU appears beneficial based on this meta-analysis. You should consider advocating for or implementing such a model if your department lacks one, as it may help reduce both ICU transfers and overall hospital stay duration. Remember that this is a synthesis of existing data, so local resource limitations must guide implementation.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized the current literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesized the current literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes. The authors categorized several approaches, with dedicated critical care areas, such as an ED-ICU setup, being the most frequently reported model. The quantitative analysis provides supportive evidence suggesting that establishing these specialized, dedicated critical care zones within the ED environment may confer benefits. Specifically, the findings indicate a potential reduction in both subsequent ICU admission rates and overall hospital length of stay for patients presenting to the ED with critically ill status when compared against other established delivery models. This synthesis helps clarify which structural approaches are most supported by current evidence.

articles.title: Predicting Echocardiography Findings in Adults Presenting to the Emergency Department With Syncope: An External Validation of the ROMEO Score
articles.takeaway: If you have a patient presenting with syncope and are considering an echo, remember that a zero ROMEO score suggests a very low probability of needing significant cardiac workup on TTE. You can use this tool to safely de-escalate testing for low-risk patients, reserving the echo resources for those who might benefit most from the study. However, always keep in mind that this is a predictive model and clinical judgment remains paramount.
articles.shortSummary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting with syncope will have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE).
articles.summary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting with syncope will have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE). The authors found that the score demonstrates robust predictive performance across different cohorts. Specifically, achieving a zero ROMEO score was associated with very high negative predictive value, reaching 98.6%. This suggests that for patients scoring low on this index, the likelihood of finding significant structural or functional cardiac abnormalities via TTE is quite low. Therefore, the score offers a practical method to stratify risk in the busy emergency department setting.

articles.title: PulmCrit: Is oseltamivir assassinating ICU patients?
articles.takeaway: Given the preliminary nature and high-risk signal from REMAP-CAP, exercise extreme caution when considering oseltamivir in critically ill patients. While further review of the full data is necessary before making definitive changes, these results strongly suggest that current institutional protocols should be reviewed to potentially de-escalate or halt routine use until more robust analyses are available.
articles.shortSummary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data concerning the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming.
articles.summary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data concerning the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming. The primary analysis presented suggests a substantial association between oseltamivir administration and increased mortality risk in this vulnerable population. Specifically, the reported likelihood of causing increased death was calculated at 98.3%. Given these preliminary results, clinicians should treat this data with caution but recognize that it warrants immediate attention regarding current guidelines for antiviral use in the ICU setting.

articles.title: Shift Pearls: Anatomic Urinary Obstruction and Septic Shock
articles.takeaway: When you suspect septic shock in a patient with potential urological compromise, remember that an obstructing process could be contributing to poor perfusion or worsening acidosis. While specific management details are pending, maintain a high index of suspicion for obstruction and consider serial urinalysis/imaging if the clinical picture is ambiguous. Be cautious about aggressive fluid resuscitation alone without addressing underlying mechanical causes.
articles.shortSummary: This 'Shift Pearls' installment is flagging the critical intersection of anatomic urinary obstruction and septic shock, a common but potentially complex scenario in the ED.
articles.summary: This 'Shift Pearls' installment is flagging the critical intersection of anatomic urinary obstruction and septic shock, a common but potentially complex scenario in the ED. While the provided excerpt only announces the topic rather than detailing specific findings or management algorithms, it signals that practical pearls are forthcoming regarding urological issues complicating sepsis. This combination demands high vigilance because acute urinary retention or obstruction can significantly worsen hemodynamic instability already present due to systemic infection. Understanding the interplay between impaired renal outflow and distributive shock is key for optimizing resuscitation efforts.

articles.title: Case Report: When Sore Throat Is More Than Just a Pain in the Neck
articles.takeaway: If a patient presents with persistent postoperative sore throat following intubation, don't just assume it's routine pharyngitis; perform a thorough oropharyngeal exam specifically checking the uvula for white necrotic tips. If you suspect this rare process, prompt consultation and further workup are warranted because delaying diagnosis can have serious consequences.
articles.shortSummary: This case report serves as an important reminder that persistent postoperative sore throat should prompt consideration of rare, but serious, complications like uvular necrosis, rather than being dismissed as simple phar…
articles.summary: This case report serves as an important reminder that persistent postoperative sore throat should prompt consideration of rare, but serious, complications like uvular necrosis, rather than being dismissed as simple pharyngitis. The authors emphasize that the diagnosis remains primarily clinical, necessitating a detailed history and physical examination when symptoms linger beyond 24 hours without improvement following intubation. A key diagnostic maneuver highlighted is the careful oropharyngeal inspection specifically looking for white necrotic tips on the uvula. While the mechanism linking sore throat to necrosis isn't fully elucidated here, recognizing this constellation of signs in an ED setting can prevent overlooking a significant airway complication.

articles.title: Infant with seizure
articles.takeaway: When evaluating infants post-trauma with concerning signs like fontanelle tension or seizures, remember that bedside cranial ultrasound can be highly diagnostic. Look for multilayered fluid collections; the absence of traversing vessels within these collections and evidence of cortical vein displacement strongly favors an SDH over SAH or hygromas. This approach helps triage imaging needs effectively.
articles.shortSummary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting after trauma with concerning neurological signs, specifically irritability and tonic posturing following a fa…
articles.summary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting after trauma with concerning neurological signs, specifically irritability and tonic posturing following a fall. The 5-month-old patient presented with physical exam findings suggestive of intracranial pathology, including ecchymoses and a tense anterior fontanelle. Ultrasound was instrumental in differentiating between potential diagnoses such as subdural hematomas (SDH), subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH), or hygromas. The characteristic appearance of bilateral, heterogeneous, multilayered collections on ultrasound, coupled with the absence of traversing vessels within these collections and evidence of displaced cortical veins, strongly supported an SDH diagnosis over other differentials.

articles.title: ECG Cases 62 – ACLS Arrhythmia Pitfalls, Part 5: Stable Narrow Complex Tachycardias
articles.takeaway: When faced with a stable narrow complex tachycardia, don't settle for the most obvious diagnosis; systematically rule out atrial fibrillation and flutter alongside sinus tachycardia. Always keep secondary causes in mind, as these can dictate management more than the underlying rhythm itself. Remember that accurate differentiation is key to avoiding unnecessary or harmful interventions.
articles.shortSummary: This resource dives into the common pitfalls encountered when interpreting ECGs in patients presenting with stable narrow complex tachycardias, which is a frequent source of diagnostic confusion in the ED.
articles.summary: This resource dives into the common pitfalls encountered when interpreting ECGs in patients presenting with stable narrow complex tachycardias, which is a frequent source of diagnostic confusion in the ED. The authors use eight real-world case examples to walk through the nuances required to accurately differentiate between sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). Beyond just pattern recognition, the discussion stresses the critical importance of considering secondary causes for these tachyarrhythmias. Ultimately, the goal is to guide the reader toward making safe management decisions based on a thorough workup rather than relying solely on an initial rhythm strip interpretation.

articles.title: Frailty stratification in the emergency department using the triage frailty and comorbidity tool
articles.takeaway: Consider using the TFC tool during triage for seemingly minor complaints to proactively identify frailty risk in low-acuity patients. If positive, these individuals may benefit from earlier assessment without disrupting overall acuity flow. Remember that TFC positivity is also a marker suggesting increased downstream resource utilization like hospitalization.
articles.shortSummary: This article explores integrating the Triage Frailty and Comorbidity (TFC) Tool into routine triage processes for low-acuity patients within the emergency department setting.
articles.summary: This article explores integrating the Triage Frailty and Comorbidity (TFC) Tool into routine triage processes for low-acuity patients within the emergency department setting. The core finding suggests that incorporating this tool helps identify vulnerable, frail individuals who might otherwise be overlooked in standard acuity sorting. Specifically, its implementation was linked to reduced waiting times for medical assessment among these high-risk, low-acuity groups. Importantly, the authors noted that this improved care for the frail did not negatively impact the established system of acuity prioritization. Furthermore, TFC positivity itself correlated with a higher likelihood of subsequent hospital admission.

articles.title: Acute urinary retention presenting as inferior pseudo-STEMI: A case report
articles.takeaway: When you see isolated, reversible inferior ST-segment elevation in a patient who is otherwise stable and lacks clear signs of ischemia or hemodynamic instability, strongly rule out massive urinary retention. Simple catheterization can resolve the changes entirely, making this a crucial, non-cardiac differential diagnosis to consider early.
articles.shortSummary: This case report details an instance where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI).
articles.summary: This case report details an instance where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). The authors highlight that the characteristic inferior ST-segment changes resolved completely following simple decompression with a Foley catheter, notably in the absence of typical hemodynamic signs like tachycardia or hypertension. This underscores that profound urinary retention can generate reversible electrical abnormalities indistinguishable from acute coronary syndrome on initial assessment. It serves as an important reminder to consider mechanical causes for ECG findings before committing to invasive cardiac workups.

articles.title: Validation of the 2022 German trauma team activation criteria: a national registry study with focus on geriatric-specific modifiers
articles.takeaway: When managing older trauma patients, remember that the 2022 TTA criteria with geriatric modifiers appear to offer superior predictive accuracy for mortality risk compared to baseline scoring. This suggests a more nuanced approach is warranted when deciding on the level of trauma resource activation. However, be aware that this validation does not quantify overtriage rates, so use these guidelines as one piece of evidence in your local resource allocation decision-making.
articles.shortSummary: This national registry study validates the updated 2022 German trauma team activation (TTA) criteria, paying special attention to how geriatric-specific modifiers impact risk stratification.
articles.summary: This national registry study validates the updated 2022 German trauma team activation (TTA) criteria, paying special attention to how geriatric-specific modifiers impact risk stratification. The authors found that incorporating these modifications significantly improves the concordance between initial triage assessments and actual observed mortality risk, which is a key metric for system performance. This improvement appears particularly pronounced within the elderly population, suggesting the updated guidelines offer better predictive power for this group compared to previous standards. While the study confirms improved alignment, it also points toward future research needing to quantify the real-world implications regarding overtriage rates and overall resource utilization across the trauma system.

articles.title: Outcomes of Acute PE Treated With DOACs in the Veterans Affairs Health System: A Retrospective Cohort Study
articles.takeaway: For low-risk acute PE patients (sPESI 0 or 1), do not default to hospitalization if the patient is otherwise stable; outpatient management appears noninferior for 30-day mortality. Remember that this finding specifically pertains to low-risk groups, and higher sPESI scores likely warrant more aggressive inpatient consideration. This suggests a strong case for streamlining care pathways to reduce unnecessary resource utilization.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S.
articles.summary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S. population presenting with acute pulmonary embolism (PE) who were managed with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs). The authors stratified their analysis based on the Wells' criteria equivalent, using the sPESI score to categorize risk. Notably, for patients classified as low-risk, specifically those with an sPESI of 0 or 1, the study found that undergoing hospitalization did not confer a survival benefit compared to being managed in the outpatient setting. Given that two-thirds of these low-risk patients were still admitted despite this finding, the results strongly advocate for reevaluating the necessity of short-stay hospitalizations when no other compelling admission criteria are present.

articles.title: No Pause, No Problem? Using Doppler Ultrasound to Detect ROSC Without Pausing Compressions for Pulse Check
articles.takeaway: If you are in a setting where Doppler ultrasound is readily available, consider its utility to assess for ROSC without interrupting compressions for pulse checks. The ability to maintain continuous chest compression quality by using flow signals warrants consideration, but remember that this remains an evaluation of diagnostic accuracy and should not replace established ACLS algorithms or clinical judgment.
articles.shortSummary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during cardiac arrest care, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality CPR efforts.
articles.summary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during cardiac arrest care, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality CPR efforts. The core concept explored is leveraging femoral arterial Doppler ultrasound waveforms obtained *during* active compressions to detect signs of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). Instead of pausing chest compressions every two minutes for a traditional pulse check, the authors evaluate if detecting pulsatility and anterograde flow signals can reliably predict true cardiac activity. If validated, this represents a significant workflow improvement by maintaining continuous, uninterrupted high-quality CPR monitoring, which is paramount for optimizing resuscitation outcomes.

articles.title: Patterns of emergency department visits for suicidal attempts associated with toxic ingestion: A retrospective cross-sectional study
articles.takeaway: When managing a patient presenting with intentional overdose in the ED, anticipate polypharmacy involving CNS depressants as common agents. Given this complexity, prompt toxicology consultation is non-negotiable for guiding antidotal therapy and monitoring for multi-system effects. Remember that optimizing care involves coordinating both acute stabilization and risk assessment.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective analysis characterizing intentional self-poisoning presentations in Israel highlights a pattern where medication overdose is the most common etiology, frequently involving polypharmacy, particularly w…
articles.summary: This retrospective analysis characterizing intentional self-poisoning presentations in Israel highlights a pattern where medication overdose is the most common etiology, frequently involving polypharmacy, particularly with central nervous system depressants. The authors emphasize that managing these complex ingestions requires more than just standard clinical care; early and robust toxicology consultation is crucial for guiding definitive treatment pathways and improving patient outcomes. Furthermore, the report underscores the broader role of medical toxicologists, extending their purview beyond immediate stabilization to include surveillance, prevention efforts, and addressing the psychological aspects of self-harm risk.

articles.title: Status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy presenting to the emergency department: Predictors and mortality risk
articles.takeaway: Given the significant association between SE in this population and increased mortality, consider systematically assessing all incoming epilepsy patients for underlying triggers. Pay extra attention to those who are elderly, have known metabolic issues, or present with signs suggestive of CNS infection, as these groups warrant heightened suspicion for impending or ongoing seizures. Early risk stratification is key here.
articles.shortSummary: This review looked at the prevalence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) specifically within a cohort of epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department in Malaysia.
articles.summary: This review looked at the prevalence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) specifically within a cohort of epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department in Malaysia. The authors found that SE was quite common, occurring in nearly one-quarter of this population, and critically, it was strongly associated with elevated in-hospital mortality rates. This suggests that simply having an epilepsy diagnosis does not preclude the risk of severe status epilepticus upon presentation. The findings emphasize the utility of identifying high-risk subgroups for proactive management. Specifically, the data point toward older age, metabolic derangements, or suspicion of a central nervous system infection as key areas for enhanced vigilance and early risk stratification.

Remember that while the model shows high sensitivity for 'low risk,' it is based on internal validation and requires external testing before changing standard diagnostic pathways. Clinicians should interpret these scores as adjuncts to physical exam findings, not replacements.

editions.headline: Fluid Wars, Late Stroke Care, and Outpatient PE Pathways
editions.summary:
The debate over initial resuscitation in septic shock continues to generate conflicting signals. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial suggests that rigid adherence to either aggressive fluid loading or immediate vasopressor use may not confer a survival advantage when comparing the two strategies head-to-head.

Shifting focus across acuity levels, we see practical shifts in management pathways. For acute ischemic stroke presenting significantly past 24 hours, advanced imaging combined with careful patient selection might still justify endovascular intervention to improve functional outcomes, though caution remains warranted due to mixed overall data. Furthermore, for low-risk patients presenting with acute PE managed on DOACs, the evidence suggests that inpatient admission may not confer a survival benefit over robust outpatient management.

Beyond resuscitation and thromboembolism, specialized tools are emerging: bedside cranial ultrasound is proving invaluable in differentiating intracranial collections in pediatric trauma, while TEE shows promise for rapidly diagnosing etiologies following non-traumatic OHCA. These varied findings—from refining the initial fluid bolus to optimizing post-discharge care—underscore that today's reading set demands a highly nuanced approach, prioritizing local protocols and patient status over sweeping algorithmic mandates.
editions.notes: Today’s reads emphasize using high-yield data points to refine existing pathways rather than overhaul them entirely.

At the bedside, remember that current data do not support a clear advantage for initiating vasopressors immediately with limited fluids versus using higher fluid loads first in septic shock. Always consider the patient's hemodynamic status and institutional protocols when titrating initial therapy.
